description:
A backup-operator wants to practise a very small “test restore” that involves
    starting a throw-away background job, recording its PID, and then shutting it
    down again.  
    Your goal is to automate that drill and leave behind a tiny audit trail that
    can be checked automatically.

    1.  Make sure the directory  
        /home/user/restore_test  
        exists (create it if it is missing).

    2.  Start a background **dummy restore job**.  
        •  Use the command  sleep 120  (the exact runtime is not important, but
           using sleep keeps the exercise harmless).  
        •  Run it in the background and capture the PID that the shell prints
           out.

    3.  Immediately after the background job starts, create the file  
        /home/user/restore_test/restore_session.log  
        and write the two lines shown below, replacing <PID> with the actual
        number you captured.  Do not add extra spaces before or after the “=”:

        PID=<PID>
        STATUS=RUNNING

    4.  Terminate the dummy restore job **gracefully** by sending it SIGTERM
        (plain kill <PID> is fine).

    5.  Append a third line to
        /home/user/restore_test/restore_session.log
        so that, when you are finished, the file contains exactly three lines in
        this exact order:

        PID=<PID>
        STATUS=RUNNING
        STATUS=TERMINATED

        •  The <PID> on the first line must be the same number that was really
           used.  
        •  The second and third lines must be the words shown above in all
           capitals and with no surrounding whitespace.

    6.  Display the finished log file to the terminal (cat is fine) so the
        operator can see the result.

    Automated tests will check that:

    •  /home/user/restore_test/restore_session.log exists.  
    •  The file has exactly three non-empty lines.  
    •  Line 1 begins with “PID=” followed immediately by a purely numeric PID.  
    •  Lines 2 and 3 are exactly “STATUS=RUNNING” and “STATUS=TERMINATED”,
       respectively.  
    •  The PID recorded in the file is no longer present in the process table
       when the task is finished.

    Completing the task should only need 2–3 commands plus a cat, but use any
    safe approach you like.
truth:
The grading script will:

    1.  Verify the directory /home/user/restore_test exists.

    2.  Read /home/user/restore_test/restore_session.log and expect:

        a.  Exactly three lines (no blank trailing line).  
        b.  Line 1 must match the regex ^PID=[0-9]+$  
            •  The captured PID value will be stored as $PID.  
        c.  Line 2 must be literally STATUS=RUNNING  
        d.  Line 3 must be literally STATUS=TERMINATED

    3.  Use ps -p $PID to confirm that the process is no longer running
        (exit-status 1 or “pid … does not exist”).

    4.  Fail the test if any of the above checks do not pass exactly.
